---
title: "Body surface area indexing can reverse the apparent direction of creatinine-based estimated glomerular filtration rate change 12 months after bariatric surgery"
format:
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-width: 7
    fig-height: 5
    fig-dpi: 300
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'sans-serif',
    'font.size': 10,
    'axes.titlesize': 13,
    'axes.titleweight': 'bold',
    'axes.labelsize': 11,
    'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'axes.facecolor': 'white',
    'axes.grid': True,
    'grid.alpha': 0.2,
    'grid.linestyle': '--',
})

ARRUDA = '#2E86AB'
GALVAO = '#A23B72'
POOLED = '#F18F01'

df = pd.read_csv('Y:/Base Rafael do Sergio/ESTUDO_C_DATASET_v4.csv', low_memory=False)
```

**Authors:** [Author list to be completed]

**Affiliations:** [To be completed]

**Corresponding author:** [To be completed]

**Word count:** ~3,400 (target: 3,500 for NDT Original Article)

**Running title:** BSA indexing artifact after bariatric surgery

---

## Key Learning Points

1. Standard indexed eGFR (mL/min/1.73 m^2^) rose by +3.2 mL/min at 12 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, but absolute (nonindexed) eGFR fell by --17.2 mL/min over the same interval, producing clinically opposite conclusions about post-surgical kidney function trajectory.

2. Shapley decomposition attributed 86% [95% CI 80--94%] of the nonindexed eGFR decline to the change in body surface area alone, confirming that the discrepancy is primarily an algebraic rescaling artifact rather than a change in true glomerular filtration.

3. Clinicians and researchers interpreting eGFR after bariatric surgery should consider reporting both indexed and nonindexed values; exclusive reliance on indexed eGFR may mask a substantial decline in absolute renal function.

---

## Abstract

**Background and Aims.** We linked routinely collected clinical and laboratory data from two bariatric surgery cohorts in Brasilia, Brazil (2008--2024), to examine how body surface area (BSA) indexing changes the interpretation of creatinine-based estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) trajectories 12 months after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB). Because BSA falls substantially after surgery, this rescaling could distort or even reverse the apparent direction of eGFR change.

**Method.** We conducted a retrospective dual-cohort study using administrative and clinical data from two independent surgical practices in Brasilia, Brazil, sharing a single reference laboratory (Sabin). The discovery cohort (Arruda; N = 268 indexed, 154 nonindexed at 12 months) used national-ID linkage; the replication cohort (Galvao; N = 283 indexed, 96 nonindexed) used deterministic name linkage. eGFR was computed with the CKD-EPI 2021 race-free equation. Nonindexed eGFR was calculated as eGFR~indexed~ x BSA~DuBois~ / 1.73. We decomposed the change in nonindexed eGFR into an eGFR component and a BSA component using a Shapley-type bilinear identity, with 5,000-replicate stratified bootstrap confidence intervals. Directional discordance was defined as the proportion of patients with indexed eGFR increase and simultaneous nonindexed eGFR decrease.

**Results.** In the discovery cohort, indexed eGFR rose (+3.60 [95% CI 1.48, 5.72]) while nonindexed eGFR fell (--16.19 [--19.49, --12.89]) at 12 months. An independent linked cohort replicated the marked nonindexed decline (--18.70 [--20.92, --16.47]) and large indexed--nonindexed divergence; after temporal validation, indexed change attenuated whereas nonindexed decline persisted. The BSA component accounted for 86.1% [79.6, 93.8] of the nonindexed decline. Nearly half of patients showed directional discordance (48.8% [42.7, 55.0]; 0% reverse pattern). Cohort-by-time interaction was non-significant (p = 0.83), confirming trajectory homogeneity.

**Conclusion.** BSA indexing can reverse the apparent direction of creatinine-based eGFR change after RYGB. Reporting only indexed eGFR may falsely reassure clinicians about post-bariatric kidney function.

---

## Introduction

Bariatric surgery, particularly Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB), produces substantial and sustained weight loss, with well-documented improvements in type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular risk [@sjostrom2012; @adams2017]. The effect of bariatric surgery on kidney function, however, remains debated. Most studies report estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) in its standard indexed form (mL/min/1.73 m^2^), which normalises filtration to a fixed body surface area (BSA) of 1.73 m^2^ [@dubois1916].

This normalisation is unproblematic when body size is approximately average. After bariatric surgery, however, patients lose 25--40% of excess weight, and BSA falls by 0.25--0.40 m^2^ within the first year. Because nonindexed (absolute) eGFR equals indexed eGFR multiplied by (BSA / 1.73), a large drop in BSA mechanically reduces absolute eGFR even if indexed eGFR remains stable or rises. In principle, this rescaling could be large enough to reverse the apparent direction of eGFR change [@delanaye2017].

Despite recognition of this issue in nephrology guidelines [@kdigo2024], no study has formally quantified the BSA indexing artifact after bariatric surgery in a large cohort, nor decomposed the relative contribution of true filtration change versus BSA rescaling.

We addressed this gap in a dual-cohort discovery--replication design, using two independent RYGB cohorts sharing the same reference laboratory to (i) quantify the divergence between indexed and nonindexed eGFR at 12 months, (ii) decompose the nonindexed eGFR change into filtration and BSA components, and (iii) test whether the artifact is reproducible across independent surgical practices.

## Methods

### Study design and reporting

This retrospective observational study used routinely collected clinical and laboratory data and followed the STROBE statement and RECORD extension for studies conducted using routinely collected health data [@benchimol2015]. The study was registered at the Open Science Framework prior to submission.

### Data sources and linkage

Two independent surgical practices in Brasilia, DF, Brazil, contributed data. Both practices use Sabin Laboratorios as their reference laboratory, enabling standardised creatinine measurements across cohorts.

**Discovery cohort (Arruda).** The Arruda cohort comprised 1,869 consecutive RYGB patients from a single bariatric surgeon's practice. Surgical technique was confirmed as 100% RYGB by the operating surgeon. Laboratory data were linked to clinical records using the Brazilian national identifier (CPF), providing gold-standard deterministic linkage. Sex was recorded in clinical charts with 100% coverage.

**Replication cohort (Galvao).** The Galvao cohort comprised 10,872 patients from a second bariatric surgeon's practice. Laboratory data from Sabin were linked to clinical records using hierarchical deterministic name linkage: exact normalised-name match (tier 1; N = 2,275) and birth-year disambiguation of homonymous names (tier 2; N = 19, all manually reviewed). The primary analysis used tiers 1 and 2 only. Patients were filtered to bypass procedures (98.5% of cohort). Sex was inferred from first names using a validated catalogue of 2,782 Brazilian names with perfect agreement against the Arruda ground-truth sample.

### Study population

The analytical dataset included patients from both cohorts with: (i) confirmed RYGB procedure; (ii) defined sex; (iii) known date of birth; and (iv) at least one valid serum creatinine measurement. The final dataset comprised 3,254 patients (1,869 Arruda; 1,385 Galvao tier 1+2 bypass).

### Measurements and definitions

**eGFR (indexed)** was calculated using the CKD-EPI 2021 race-free equation [@inker2021]. Serum creatinine values outside 0.20--5.0 mg/dL were excluded.

**BSA** was calculated using the DuBois formula. Height in the Galvao cohort was recorded in metres and converted to centimetres. Sensitivity analysis used the Mosteller formula.

**Nonindexed eGFR** was calculated as eGFR~indexed~ x BSA~DuBois~ / 1.73. This required observed weight at both pre-operative and 12-month time points; no weight imputation was used.

**Time windows.** Pre-operative creatinine was defined as the measurement closest to surgery within --365 to --1 days. The 12-month window was 301--548 days post-surgery. Multiple measurements within a window were resolved by selecting the value closest to the window centre.

**Age at examination** used a three-tier priority system: actual exam date, days-from-surgery offset, or age-at-surgery plus window offset. The maximum age error was 0.045 years (0.03 mL/min eGFR impact).

### Shapley decomposition

We decomposed the change in nonindexed eGFR into two additive components using the bilinear Shapley identity:

$$\Delta \text{eGFR}_{\text{NI}} = \underbrace{\Delta \text{eGFR}_{\text{idx}} \times \frac{\overline{\text{BSA}}}{1.73}}_{\text{eGFR component}} + \underbrace{\overline{\text{eGFR}}_{\text{idx}} \times \frac{\Delta \text{BSA}}{1.73}}_{\text{BSA component}}$$

where $\overline{\text{BSA}}$ and $\overline{\text{eGFR}}_{\text{idx}}$ denote the mean of pre-operative and 12-month values. This decomposition is algebraically exact (zero residual). Bootstrap 95% confidence intervals used 5,000 patient-level replicates, stratified by cohort in pooled analyses.

### Directional discordance

We computed the proportion of patients with simultaneous indexed eGFR increase (>0) and nonindexed eGFR decrease (<0), with Wilson score 95% confidence intervals.

### Statistical analysis

Within-person changes were tested with paired t-tests. Between-cohort heterogeneity was assessed by interaction p-values. A linear mixed-effects model with restricted cubic spline (3 knots at the 10th, 50th, and 90th percentiles of time) and random intercept per patient was fit to assess temporal trajectory and cohort homogeneity. Sensitivity analyses included: sex-stratified, BMI-stratified, BSA formula (Mosteller), creatinine range (0.4--3.0), pre-operative window restriction (--180 days, --90 days), tight 12-month window (365 +/- 45 days), and date-validated Galvao 12-month subset. G-category transitions were assessed using indexed eGFR thresholds without albuminuria (termed "G-category" rather than "KDIGO stage"). All analyses used Python 3.13 with scipy, statsmodels, pandas, and numpy.

## Results

```{python}
#| label: fig-flowchart
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1. Study flow diagram (STROBE/RECORD). Two independent bariatric surgery cohorts were linked to a shared reference laboratory. The analytical dataset was derived through hierarchical deterministic linkage, quality filters, and time-window assignment."

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 12))
ax.set_xlim(0, 100)
ax.set_ylim(0, 100)
ax.axis('off')

box_kw = dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.5', edgecolor='#333333', linewidth=1.2)
arr_kw = dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#333333', linewidth=1.5)

def box(ax, x, y, w, h, text, fc='#E8F4FD', fs=8, bold=False):
    rect = mpatches.FancyBboxPatch((x - w/2, y - h/2), w, h,
                                    boxstyle='round,pad=0.4', facecolor=fc,
                                    edgecolor='#333333', linewidth=1.2)
    ax.add_patch(rect)
    weight = 'bold' if bold else 'normal'
    ax.text(x, y, text, ha='center', va='center', fontsize=fs, fontweight=weight,
            wrap=True, linespacing=1.3)

def arrow(ax, x1, y1, x2, y2):
    ax.annotate('', xy=(x2, y2), xytext=(x1, y1),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='#333333', linewidth=1.5))

def side_box(ax, x, y, w, h, text, fc='#FDE8E8', fs=7):
    box(ax, x, y, w, h, text, fc=fc, fs=fs)

# Title
ax.text(50, 98, 'Figure 1. Study Flow Diagram (STROBE/RECORD)', ha='center', va='top',
        fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

# --- LEFT SIDE: ARRUDA ---
ax.text(25, 95, 'DISCOVERY (Arruda)', ha='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color=ARRUDA)

box(ax, 25, 90, 22, 4, 'Clinical records\nN = 1,869 RYGB patients', fc='#D4E8F7', fs=8, bold=True)
arrow(ax, 25, 88, 25, 85)

box(ax, 25, 82.5, 22, 4, 'CPF linkage to\nSabin laboratory\n(gold standard)', fc='#E8F4FD', fs=7.5)
arrow(ax, 25, 80.5, 25, 77.5)

box(ax, 25, 75, 22, 4, 'Quality filters:\nsex, DOB, valid Cr\nN = 1,869', fc='#E8F4FD', fs=7.5)
arrow(ax, 25, 73, 25, 70)

box(ax, 25, 67.5, 22, 4, 'Paired PRE + 12M\nindexed eGFR\nN = 268', fc='#C8E6C9', fs=8, bold=True)
arrow(ax, 25, 65.5, 25, 62.5)

box(ax, 25, 60, 22, 4, 'Paired PRE + 12M\nnonindexed eGFR\n(observed weight)\nN = 154', fc='#A5D6A7', fs=8, bold=True)

# --- RIGHT SIDE: GALVAO ---
ax.text(75, 95, 'REPLICATION (Galvao)', ha='center', fontsize=10, fontweight='bold', color=GALVAO)

box(ax, 75, 90, 22, 4, 'Clinical records\nN = 10,872 patients', fc='#F3DFF1', fs=8, bold=True)
arrow(ax, 75, 88, 75, 85)

box(ax, 75, 82.5, 22, 5, 'Sabin laboratory\nN = 6,236 patients', fc='#F8E8F6', fs=7.5)
arrow(ax, 75, 80, 75, 77)

box(ax, 75, 74.5, 22, 5, 'Name linkage:\nTier 1 exact: 2,281\nTier 2 homonym: 19\nTotal: 2,300', fc='#F8E8F6', fs=7.5)

# Exclusion box
side_box(ax, 96, 74.5, 8, 5, 'Excluded:\nTier 3: 368\nTier 4: 581\n(sensitivity\nonly)', fc='#FDE8E8', fs=6.5)

arrow(ax, 75, 72, 75, 69)

box(ax, 75, 66.5, 22, 4, 'Bypass filter +\nquality filters\nN = 1,385', fc='#F8E8F6', fs=7.5)
arrow(ax, 75, 64.5, 75, 61.5)

box(ax, 75, 59, 22, 4, 'Paired PRE + 12M\nindexed eGFR\nN = 283', fc='#C8E6C9', fs=8, bold=True)
arrow(ax, 75, 57, 75, 54)

box(ax, 75, 51.5, 22, 4, 'Paired PRE + 12M\nnonindexed eGFR\n(observed weight)\nN = 96', fc='#A5D6A7', fs=8, bold=True)

# --- BOTTOM: COMBINED ---
arrow(ax, 25, 58, 38, 43)
arrow(ax, 75, 49.5, 62, 43)

box(ax, 50, 40, 30, 5, 'COMBINED ANALYTICAL DATASET\nN = 3,254 patients\nIndexed 12M: 551 | Nonindexed 12M: 250',
    fc='#FFF3E0', fs=8.5, bold=True)
arrow(ax, 50, 37.5, 50, 34)

# Analysis boxes
box(ax, 25, 30, 20, 6, 'PRIMARY ANALYSIS\n\n12M paired changes\nShapley decomposition\nDirectional discordance',
    fc='#E3F2FD', fs=7.5, bold=True)
box(ax, 50, 30, 20, 6, 'LME MODEL\n\n3,494 obs / 1,573 pts\nRCS 3 knots\nCohort x time p=0.83',
    fc='#E3F2FD', fs=7.5, bold=True)
box(ax, 75, 30, 20, 6, 'SENSITIVITY\n\nSex, BMI, Mosteller\nCr range, PRE window\nDate-validated Galvao',
    fc='#E3F2FD', fs=7.5, bold=True)

arrow(ax, 40, 37.5, 25, 33)
arrow(ax, 50, 37.5, 50, 33)
arrow(ax, 60, 37.5, 75, 33)

# Shared lab note
box(ax, 50, 22, 35, 3.5, 'Both cohorts: Sabin Laboratorios (shared reference laboratory)\nCKD-EPI 2021 race-free | BSA DuBois | No weight imputation',
    fc='#FFFDE7', fs=7.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-baseline
#| tbl-cap: "Table 1. Baseline characteristics by cohort"

arr = df[df['COORTE'] == 'Arruda']
gal = df[df['COORTE'] == 'Galvao']

def fmt(series, fmt_str='.1f'):
    return f"{series.mean():{fmt_str}} +/- {series.std():{fmt_str}}"

def pct(series, val):
    return f"{(series == val).sum()}/{len(series)} ({(series == val).mean()*100:.1f}%)"

rows = [
    ['N', str(len(arr)), str(len(gal)), str(len(df))],
    ['Age, years', fmt(arr['IDADE_CIRURGIA']), fmt(gal['IDADE_CIRURGIA']), fmt(df['IDADE_CIRURGIA'])],
    ['Female sex, n (%)', pct(arr['SEXO'], 'Feminino'), pct(gal['SEXO'], 'Feminino'), pct(df['SEXO'], 'Feminino')],
    ['Weight, kg', fmt(arr['PESO_PRE']), fmt(gal['PESO_PRE']), fmt(df['PESO_PRE'])],
    ['Height, cm', fmt(arr['ALTURA_CM']), fmt(gal['ALTURA_CM']), fmt(df['ALTURA_CM'])],
    ['BMI, kg/m2', fmt(arr['IMC_PRE']), fmt(gal['IMC_PRE']), fmt(df['IMC_PRE'])],
    ['BSA, m2', fmt(arr['BSA_DUBOIS_PRE'], '.2f'), fmt(gal['BSA_DUBOIS_PRE'], '.2f'), fmt(df['BSA_DUBOIS_PRE'], '.2f')],
    ['Creatinine, mg/dL', fmt(arr['CREATININA_PRE'], '.2f'), fmt(gal['CREATININA_PRE'], '.2f'), fmt(df['CREATININA_PRE'], '.2f')],
    ['eGFR indexed, mL/min/1.73m2', fmt(arr['EGFR_CKD_EPI_PRE']), fmt(gal['EGFR_CKD_EPI_PRE']), fmt(df['EGFR_CKD_EPI_PRE'])],
    ['eGFR nonindexed, mL/min', fmt(arr['EGFR_NI_PRE']), fmt(gal['EGFR_NI_PRE']), fmt(df['EGFR_NI_PRE'])],
]

tbl = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=['Variable', 'Arruda (Discovery)', 'Galvao (Replication)', 'Combined'])
# Display as markdown table
print(tbl.to_markdown(index=False))
```

### Primary analysis: 12-month paired changes

In the discovery cohort (Arruda), indexed eGFR rose by +3.60 mL/min/1.73 m^2^ [95% CI 1.48, 5.72; p = 1.0 x 10^-3^] while nonindexed eGFR fell by --16.19 mL/min [--19.49, --12.89; p = 2.0 x 10^-17^]. The replication cohort (Galvao) confirmed the marked nonindexed decline (--18.70 [--20.92, --16.47; p = 1.4 x 10^-29^]) and large indexed--nonindexed divergence (21.21 mL/min). In the full replication sample, indexed eGFR also rose (+2.80 [1.74, 3.86; p = 3.8 x 10^-7^]); however, when restricted to date-validated 12-month measurements (301--548 days, N = 48), indexed change attenuated to near zero (+/--0.06, p = 0.96) while nonindexed decline persisted (--18.94 [--22.61, --15.28; p = 2.3 x 10^-8^]). Interaction p-values were non-significant for both indexed (0.50) and nonindexed (0.28) endpoints.

### Shapley decomposition

```{python}
#| label: fig-shapley
#| fig-cap: "Figure 2. Shapley decomposition of nonindexed eGFR change at 12 months. The BSA component (orange) dominates in both cohorts; the eGFR component (blue) is small and positive. Error bars represent 5,000-replicate bootstrap 95% CIs."

boot = pd.read_csv('Y:/Base Rafael do Sergio/tabelas_v4/Tab17_shapley_bootstrap.csv')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))

cohorts = ['Arruda', 'Galvao', 'Pooled']
labels = ['Arruda\n(Discovery)', 'Galvao\n(Replication)', 'Pooled']
x = np.arange(len(cohorts))
w = 0.35

egfr_vals = []
egfr_lo = []
egfr_hi = []
bsa_vals = []
bsa_lo = []
bsa_hi = []

for c in cohorts:
    e = boot[(boot['Cohort'] == c) & (boot['Metric'] == 'Comp_eGFR')].iloc[0]
    b = boot[(boot['Cohort'] == c) & (boot['Metric'] == 'Comp_BSA')].iloc[0]
    egfr_vals.append(e['Point_estimate'])
    egfr_lo.append(e['Point_estimate'] - e['CI95_lo'])
    egfr_hi.append(e['CI95_hi'] - e['Point_estimate'])
    bsa_vals.append(b['Point_estimate'])
    bsa_lo.append(b['Point_estimate'] - b['CI95_lo'])
    bsa_hi.append(b['CI95_hi'] - b['Point_estimate'])

bars1 = ax.bar(x - w/2, egfr_vals, w, label='eGFR component', color=ARRUDA,
               yerr=[egfr_lo, egfr_hi], capsize=4, edgecolor='white')
bars2 = ax.bar(x + w/2, bsa_vals, w, label='BSA component', color=POOLED,
               yerr=[bsa_lo, bsa_hi], capsize=4, edgecolor='white')

ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(labels)
ax.set_ylabel('Component (mL/min)')
ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax.legend(framealpha=0.95, loc='lower left')
ax.set_title('Shapley Decomposition of Nonindexed eGFR Change at 12 Months')

for i, (e, b) in enumerate(zip(egfr_vals, bsa_vals)):
    pct = abs(b) / (abs(e) + abs(b)) * 100
    ax.text(i, 5.5, f'BSA: {pct:.0f}%', ha='center', fontsize=9, fontweight='bold', color=POOLED)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The BSA component accounted for 84.9% [74.8, 97.2] of the nonindexed decline in Arruda, 88.1% [82.1, 94.5] in Galvao, and 86.1% [79.6, 93.8] pooled (Figure 2). The non-BSA creatinine-based component was small and positive in both cohorts, indicating that the creatinine-based filtration estimate itself did not decline.

### Directional discordance

```{python}
#| label: fig-forest
#| fig-cap: "Figure 3. Forest plot of 12-month paired changes in indexed and nonindexed eGFR, with directional discordance. Left panel: mean change with 95% CI. Right panel: directional classification of individual patients."

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 5), gridspec_kw={'width_ratios': [3, 2]})

# Forest plot
endpoints = [
    ('eGFR indexed\n(Arruda)', 3.60, 1.48, 5.72, ARRUDA),
    ('eGFR indexed\n(Galvao)', 2.80, 1.74, 3.86, GALVAO),
    ('eGFR indexed\n(Pooled)', 3.19, 2.02, 4.35, POOLED),
    ('', np.nan, np.nan, np.nan, 'white'),
    ('eGFR nonindexed\n(Arruda)', -16.19, -19.49, -12.89, ARRUDA),
    ('eGFR nonindexed\n(Galvao)', -18.70, -20.92, -16.47, GALVAO),
    ('eGFR nonindexed\n(Pooled)', -17.15, -19.36, -14.95, POOLED),
]

y_pos = np.arange(len(endpoints))[::-1]
for i, (label, mean, lo, hi, color) in enumerate(endpoints):
    if np.isnan(mean):
        continue
    ax1.errorbar(mean, y_pos[i], xerr=[[mean-lo], [hi-mean]], fmt='D', color=color,
                 markersize=8, capsize=4, capthick=1.5, linewidth=1.5)
    ax1.text(-25, y_pos[i], label, va='center', ha='right', fontsize=8, color=color)

ax1.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='-')
ax1.set_xlabel('Mean change (mL/min)')
ax1.set_xlim(-25, 10)
ax1.set_yticks([])
ax1.set_title('12-Month Paired Changes')
ax1.axhspan(2.5, 6.5, alpha=0.05, color='green')
ax1.axhspan(-0.5, 2.5, alpha=0.05, color='red')

# Discordance stacked bar
disc = pd.read_csv('Y:/Base Rafael do Sergio/tabelas_v4/Tab13_directional_discordance.csv')
categories = ['idx UP\nNI DOWN\n(discordant)', 'Both UP', 'Both DOWN', 'idx DOWN\nNI UP\n(reverse)']
colors_disc = ['#E74C3C', '#3498DB', '#95A5A6', '#2ECC71']

for idx_c, cohort in enumerate(['Arruda', 'Galvao', 'Combined']):
    row = disc[disc['Cohort'] == cohort].iloc[0]
    n = row['N']
    vals = [row['N_discordant']/n*100, row['N_both_up']/n*100,
            row['N_both_down']/n*100, row['N_reverse']/n*100]
    bottom = 0
    for j, (v, c) in enumerate(zip(vals, colors_disc)):
        bar = ax2.bar(idx_c, v, bottom=bottom, color=c, edgecolor='white', width=0.6)
        if v > 5:
            ax2.text(idx_c, bottom + v/2, f'{v:.0f}%', ha='center', va='center',
                     fontsize=8, fontweight='bold', color='white')
        bottom += v

ax2.set_xticks([0, 1, 2])
ax2.set_xticklabels(['Arruda', 'Galvao', 'Pooled'], fontsize=9)
ax2.set_ylabel('% of patients')
ax2.set_title('Directional Classification')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 105)

# Legend
legend_patches = [mpatches.Patch(color=c, label=l) for c, l in
                  zip(colors_disc, ['Discordant (idx+/NI-)', 'Both up', 'Both down', 'Reverse'])]
ax2.legend(handles=legend_patches, fontsize=7, loc='upper right', framealpha=0.9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Nearly half of patients with paired measurements showed directional discordance: 48.8% [95% CI 42.7, 55.0] had indexed eGFR increase with simultaneous nonindexed eGFR decrease (Figure 3). No patient (0/250) showed the reverse pattern (indexed decrease with nonindexed increase). The discordance was consistent across cohorts (Arruda 43.5%, Galvao 57.3%).

### Dose--response relationship

```{python}
#| label: fig-dose
#| fig-cap: "Figure 4. Dose-response relationship between BSA change and indexed--nonindexed divergence at 12 months. Each point represents one patient. The linear fit (R2 = 0.72) demonstrates that the magnitude of the indexing artifact is proportional to the degree of weight loss."

paired = df[df['DELTA_EGFR_IDX_12M'].notna() & df['DELTA_EGFR_NI_12M'].notna() &
            df['DELTA_BSA_12M'].notna()].copy()
paired['divergence'] = paired['DELTA_EGFR_IDX_12M'] - paired['DELTA_EGFR_NI_12M']

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.5))

# Panel A: DBSA vs divergence
for label, color, marker in [('Arruda', ARRUDA, 'o'), ('Galvao', GALVAO, 's')]:
    sub = paired[paired['COORTE'] == label]
    ax1.scatter(sub['DELTA_BSA_12M'], sub['divergence'], c=color, alpha=0.5,
                s=20, marker=marker, label=label, edgecolors='white', linewidth=0.3)

# Fit line
x_fit = paired['DELTA_BSA_12M'].values
y_fit = paired['divergence'].values
mask = np.isfinite(x_fit) & np.isfinite(y_fit)
slope, intercept, r, p, se = stats.linregress(x_fit[mask], y_fit[mask])
x_line = np.linspace(x_fit[mask].min(), x_fit[mask].max(), 100)
ax1.plot(x_line, slope * x_line + intercept, 'k--', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.7)
ax1.text(0.05, 0.95, f'R$^2$ = {r**2:.2f}\nrho = {stats.spearmanr(x_fit[mask], y_fit[mask])[0]:.2f}',
         transform=ax1.transAxes, va='top', fontsize=9,
         bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='wheat', alpha=0.8))

ax1.set_xlabel('Delta BSA (m2)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Divergence (mL/min)')
ax1.set_title('A. BSA Change vs Divergence')
ax1.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)

# Panel B: TWL vs divergence
twl_col = None
for c in ['TWL_12M', 'PPT_12M', '%TWL_12M']:
    if c in paired.columns:
        twl_col = c
        break

if twl_col and paired[twl_col].notna().sum() > 20:
    sub_twl = paired[paired[twl_col].notna()]
    for label, color, marker in [('Arruda', ARRUDA, 'o'), ('Galvao', GALVAO, 's')]:
        s = sub_twl[sub_twl['COORTE'] == label]
        if len(s) > 0:
            ax2.scatter(s[twl_col], s['divergence'], c=color, alpha=0.5,
                        s=20, marker=marker, label=label, edgecolors='white', linewidth=0.3)
    x2 = sub_twl[twl_col].values
    y2 = sub_twl['divergence'].values
    m2 = np.isfinite(x2) & np.isfinite(y2)
    if m2.sum() > 10:
        sl2, int2, r2, p2, se2 = stats.linregress(x2[m2], y2[m2])
        x2_line = np.linspace(x2[m2].min(), x2[m2].max(), 100)
        ax2.plot(x2_line, sl2 * x2_line + int2, 'k--', linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.7)
        ax2.text(0.05, 0.95, f'R$^2$ = {r2**2:.2f}\nrho = {stats.spearmanr(x2[m2], y2[m2])[0]:.2f}',
                 transform=ax2.transAxes, va='top', fontsize=9,
                 bbox=dict(boxstyle='round', facecolor='wheat', alpha=0.8))
    ax2.set_xlabel('%TWL at 12 months')
    ax2.set_ylabel('Divergence (mL/min)')
    ax2.set_title('B. Weight Loss vs Divergence')
    ax2.legend(fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
else:
    ax2.text(0.5, 0.5, 'TWL data\nnot available\nin combined dataset', ha='center', va='center',
             transform=ax2.transAxes, fontsize=12, color='gray')
    ax2.set_title('B. Weight Loss vs Divergence')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The divergence between indexed and nonindexed eGFR showed a strong monotonic relationship with BSA change (Spearman rho = --0.83, p < 10^-64^; R^2^ = 0.72) (Figure 4), consistent with an algebraic mechanism: greater weight loss produces greater BSA reduction, which amplifies the rescaling artifact.

### G-category stability

At 12 months, 89.1% of patients (491/551) maintained their pre-operative G-category, 9.4% improved, and 1.5% worsened. This pattern was stable through 60 months (90.8% stable, 3.1% worsened). Major indexed G-category shifts were uncommon over follow-up, although this assessment was limited by the absence of albuminuria data.

### Longitudinal mixed-effects model

The linear mixed-effects model (3,494 observations from 1,573 patients) confirmed a non-linear temporal trajectory for indexed eGFR (spline p < 10^-8^) with initial rise followed by late decline. The cohort-by-time interaction was non-significant (p = 0.83), confirming homogeneous trajectories across cohorts. Baseline age was the dominant predictor (--0.78 mL/min per year; p < 10^-163^). For nonindexed eGFR (1,862 observations, 1,236 patients), there was a strong negative association with time with additional non-linear structure (spline p < 10^-35^), dominated by the BSA component.

### Sensitivity analyses

The primary finding was robust across all pre-specified sensitivity analyses: sex-stratified (female: +3.23/--17.09; male: +2.97/--17.51), BMI-stratified (consistent across all groups), alternative BSA formula (Mosteller: nonindexed decline --21.35 vs DuBois --17.15), restricted creatinine range (0.4--3.0: consistent), pre-operative window restriction (--180 days: consistent; --90 days: consistent), tight 12-month window (365 +/- 45 days, Arruda: nonindexed --16.06), and Galvao date-validated nominal window (301--548 days: nonindexed --18.94, discordance 76.5%).

## Discussion

This dual-cohort study demonstrates that BSA indexing can reverse the apparent direction of creatinine-based eGFR change after RYGB. While indexed eGFR suggests mild improvement at 12 months, nonindexed eGFR reveals a substantial decline of approximately 17 mL/min. This discrepancy affects nearly half of individual patients and is almost entirely attributable to BSA rescaling rather than a change in the creatinine-based filtration estimate itself.

### Mechanism

The Shapley decomposition provides formal confirmation of the algebraic mechanism. At 12 months, 86% of the nonindexed decline is explained by the BSA component alone. The non-BSA creatinine-based component was small and positive, suggesting that the creatinine-based filtration estimate itself did not decline --- though this should be interpreted cautiously, as creatinine production may also change after major weight loss due to altered muscle mass [@levey2014].

### Clinical implications

The practical consequence is straightforward: a clinician reviewing indexed eGFR after bariatric surgery sees +3 mL/min ("stable or improving") while absolute renal function has fallen by 17 mL/min. This discrepancy is not a statistical curiosity --- it affects individual clinical decisions. Nearly half of patients had clinically opposite trajectories depending on which metric was used, and no patient showed the reverse pattern.

We do not argue that nonindexed eGFR is the correct metric for drug dosing or staging; this remains an area of active debate [@delanaye2017]. Rather, we demonstrate that exclusive reliance on indexed eGFR after bariatric surgery provides a systematically optimistic picture of kidney function trajectory that clinicians should be aware of.

### Strengths and limitations

Strengths include the dual-cohort design with independent discovery and replication, shared laboratory standardisation, large sample size, formal algebraic decomposition with bootstrap inference, and comprehensive sensitivity analyses.

Limitations include the absence of cystatin C-based or measured GFR, which precludes assessment of whether the creatinine-based filtration estimate itself reflects true filtration change after altered muscle mass. Albuminuria data were essentially absent, limiting classification to G-categories rather than complete KDIGO staging. In the Galvao cohort, temporal window assignment showed some misclassification (31.7% of pre-operative dates were post-surgical; 12-month window range 51--815 days), although date-validated sensitivity analysis substantially mitigated this concern (date availability remained incomplete: 104/283 with real dates, 17 nonindexed pairs in the nominal window). In the Arruda cohort, weight and laboratory data were captured within the same scheduled follow-up encounter; in the Galvao cohort, weight was recorded by the surgeon and creatinine was assayed at an independent laboratory, so a modest lag between measurements cannot be excluded, although it is unlikely to explain a divergence of approximately 20 mL/min.

Reported comorbidity prevalence differed markedly between cohorts, likely reflecting ascertainment heterogeneity across source systems; because the primary endpoint was within-person change in creatinine-based eGFR under alternative BSA scaling, these variables were not used to define or compute the primary outcome.

### Conclusion

BSA indexing can reverse the apparent direction of creatinine-based eGFR change 12 months after RYGB. Clinicians and researchers should consider reporting both indexed and nonindexed eGFR when evaluating kidney function after bariatric surgery. The current practice of reporting only indexed eGFR may falsely reassure clinicians about post-bariatric kidney function.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Supplementary Material

Available online: STROBE/RECORD checklist, complete LME coefficients, G-category transition table, Mosteller sensitivity, pre-operative window sensitivity, date-validated Galvao sensitivity, representativeness analysis, linked vs unlinked comparison.
